title: Technical: The Anti-Drift Harness
subtitle: How a set of automated helpers keeps every part of a growing codebase in agreement as an AI builds it, explained piece by piece.
abstract: An AI coding assistant does fine work on each change it makes. The problem shows up later: as a project grows, the many pieces that make up a feature, what was asked for, the database, the screens, the help text, quietly fall out of agreement with one another. We call that drift. This appendix explains the Anti-Drift Harness, a set of automated helpers that watch for drift as the work happens and flag it, rather than leaving a person to catch it in a later audit. It walks through seven approaches in plain terms, each with an animated diagram, and a pattern where the helpers run beside the project and leave notes for the builder to act on. It is a proposed design, offered for review.

slides.title: The Anti-Drift Harness
slides.caption: Technical appendix · paper 17
slides.subcaption: Keeping every part of a growing codebase in agreement as an AI builds it.
slides.text: An AI coding assistant does fine work on each change. The trouble is quieter and slower: as a project grows, the many pieces of a feature, the requirement, the database, the screens, the help text, fall out of agreement with one another. We call that drift. The Anti-Drift Harness is a set of automated helpers that watch for it as the work happens and flag it, instead of leaving a person to catch it later.

slides.title: It runs, but is it well built?
slides.caption: Where the hidden cost lives

visual_config.stat_label: of the work becomes rework that piles up over time, mostly drift between related pieces

slides.subcaption: An AI's code can be 97 to 99 percent correct and run, yet only about 90 percent well built underneath.
slides.text: An AI's code almost always runs. Underneath, it is usually, but not always, well built. That gap is the hidden cost, and most of it is drift between related pieces rather than an outright bug. When the AI changes one piece it updates a neighbour or two and moves on; the rest quietly fall out of date.

slides.title: A feature is a chain
slides.caption: From the request to the training
slides.subcaption: Change one piece and the rest must change to match.
slides.text: A feature is a chain of connected pieces, from what was asked for, through the database and the code, out to the screens, the documentation, and the staff training. Change one and the rest must change to match. Drift is what happens when they do not, and it spreads both along one feature and across features that share parts.

slides.title: Seven ways to catch it
slides.caption: Mostly automatic, simple checks

items.label: Follow the change
items.desc: Trace an edit outward and report where pieces no longer match (the Chain Walker).

items.label: Map the activity
items.desc: A heat-map of which files are changing, so checks know where to look (the Fingerprint).

items.label: Small automatic tests
items.desc: Quick checks that one pair of pieces still agrees, growing with the project (the Evals).

items.label: Notes, cards, sweeps, a board
items.desc: Structured save notes, per-file index cards, an end-of-step checklist, and a shared dashboard.

slides.text: Seven approaches, from following a single change outward to a shared dashboard of the whole project. Most of the checking is done with fixed, repeatable logic; the AI is asked to judge only where judgment is genuinely needed.

slides.title: Watchers on the side
slides.caption: Not a second builder
slides.subcaption: They observe, and leave notes the builder picks up.
slides.text: The watchers run beside the project, not inside the builder. Each observes the files, runs its checks when something changes, and leaves a note in a shared folder. The builder reads the notes at the start of its next step and handles them like any other task. Because the notes are just files, one watcher having trouble never disrupts the builder.

slides.title: Where it sits
slides.caption: Part of the collection
slides.subcaption: Under the Well-Built Harness; alongside the four review agents.
slides.text: This sits under the Well-Built Harness and works alongside the red, green, blue, and yellow review agents. It answers the same pressure the Compression Problem describes: as scale grows, detail is lost at every handoff. The harness exists so the checking runs on its own, whether or not a person remembers.

sections.title: Make the save note count (Commit Spine)
An index card per file (Dependency Manifest)
How to combine them, and what is still open

blocks.text: When a team works with an AI coding assistant under clear standards, the pace is remarkable. The trouble is quieter and slower. A real software feature is not one file; it is a chain of connected pieces: the written requirement, the database design, the program logic, the screens a user sees, the help documentation, the staff training. Change one piece and the others have to change to match. An AI is diligent about the piece in front of it, updates a neighbour or two, and moves on. The rest slip out of date. Months later the pieces disagree with one another, and the project erodes under its own changes even though every single edit looked correct. This appendix is about that gap, and the automated helpers that close it. It is a proposed design, offered to the team for review, and it sits under the [Well-Built Harness](/paper/p7p2k).

blocks.title: The drift problem

blocks.text: The fix that already works is simple discipline. Every few steps, a person pauses and asks the AI to check that everything still lines up: the documentation matches the code, the screens match the data, the tests still cover the work. The check catches the drift, and it gets fixed. The catch is that it depends on a person remembering to run it. The harness exists so that check runs on its own, every time, whether or not anyone remembers.

The reason drift is easy to miss is that the code still works. An AI's code is almost always functionally correct; it runs. It is usually, but not always, well organized underneath. That gap, between "it works" and "it is built to last," is the hidden cost, and most of it is drift between the pieces rather than an outright bug.

blocks.label: It runs, but is it well built?
blocks.body: An AI's output is typically 97 to 99 percent functionally correct; it runs. But it is only about 90 percent well built underneath. That 7 to 10 percent gap is rework that compounds over time, and most of it is drift between related pieces, not a failing test.

blocks.title: A feature is a chain

blocks.text: Picture a single feature as a chain of fourteen links. It starts with the requirement, what we asked for, runs through the database design and the program logic, out to the screens a person uses, and ends in the help documentation and the staff training. Every link depends on the ones beside it. When the AI changes one link, it usually updates the link or two right next to it, then stops. Later, when work resumes somewhere else, the AI reads those now-outdated links as if they were still true and builds on top of them. The chain breaks a little more each time. Remove a field from the database, for example, and that change has to ripple out to the screen, the documentation, and the training notes. Most of the time, it does not.

Drift travels two ways. It travels along one feature's chain, link by link. And it travels across features, when something shared, a renamed component or an outdated library, changes in one place and quietly breaks another. A good harness catches both, and it catches them while the work is happening, not in a cleanup months later. The seven approaches that follow all do this. Each one is built from small, ordinary parts: short instruction files the AI follows (called skills), automatic triggers that fire at set moments (called hooks), and quick automated checks (called evals). None of it is exotic.

blocks.title: The habit, made automatic

blocks.text: The whole idea is just the manual habit, pause, check, fix, turned into something the project does for itself. The mental checklist becomes a set of automatic checks. The pause becomes a trigger that fires when the AI finishes a step. And the work is split across three kinds of helper: a builder that writes the application, quick background scripts that constantly gather evidence, and an anti-drift reviewer that reads that evidence on its own slower rhythm and decides whether something is worth flagging. As the project grows, new links appear and new checks are added beside them. The discipline stops living in one person's memory and becomes a property of the project itself.

blocks.title: Follow the change (Chain Walker)

blocks.text: The Chain Walker follows a change outward along the chain. The moment the AI edits a file, the walker starts at that file and steps to its neighbours in both directions: the pieces that feed into it and the pieces that depend on it. At each step it compares the two ends. Do the names, the fields, the promises still match? Where they do not, it records exactly what fell out of line. It keeps walking to a set distance and produces one tidy report of what drifted. Its advantage is reach: it checks the whole chain rather than the one or two links beside the change, and its findings are specific enough that a reviewer can see precisely what no longer agrees.

blocks.title: A change spreads one step, then drift builds up
blocks.caption: The fourteen links of a feature shown as columns. A change lands and spreads to its immediate neighbours, then stops. The links it should have reached but did not are later flagged as drift, and the watcher marks them and leaves notes.

blocks.title: Follow the change, in plain terms
rows: Follows a change outward along the chain and reports where the pieces no longer match.
The moment the AI saves a change to a file, and once more at the end of each step.
A short report saved beside the code, listing exactly what drifted.
Catching drift everywhere along the chain, with specific, checkable findings.
It does real work on every edit, so it has a cost; and someone has to define what "matching" means for each kind of link.

blocks.title: Map the activity (Fingerprint Heatmap)

blocks.text: This one does not judge anything; it just keeps a record of where the work is happening. Every time the AI touches a file, it adds a tally. A simple heat-map then shows the project as a grid of tiles, with the busiest files glowing brightest and the most recent change marked. On its own it finds no drift. What it gives you is a fingerprint of where the action has been, so the other, smarter checks know where to look first. It is cheap, needs no setup, and is useful on day one.

blocks.title: Where the work has been happening
blocks.caption: Files grouped by folder; each tile glows brighter the more it is changed. New edits flash, then settle. The busiest files are marked as hotspots for the other checks to look at first.

blocks.title: Map the activity, in plain terms
rows: Keeps a running tally of which files are being changed and shows it as a heat-map.
Quietly, on every edit; the picture is drawn whenever you ask for it.
A simple log and a shareable picture of where the work happened.
Seeing the hotspots at a glance and pointing the other checks at them. Works from day one.
It only shows activity; it does not catch drift by itself, so pair it with one that does.

blocks.title: Small automatic tests (Chain Evals)

blocks.text: A check here is just a small, automatic test for one specific kind of mismatch. Does the database design match the script that builds it? Does the published interface match its documentation? Each test reads both ends and reports any difference. The tests live right next to the code and grow with the project: when a new kind of link appears, you add a test for it. Whenever the AI changes a file, only the tests that touch that file run. They are fast, they give the same answer every time, and because the team writes them, they catch exactly the mistakes this project tends to make.

blocks.title: Small checks, run after run
blocks.caption: Each row is a pair of pieces that should agree; each column is a recent run. Green passed, amber is a warning, red failed. A failed check leaves a note.

blocks.title: Small automatic tests, in plain terms
rows: Runs small automatic tests, each checking that one specific pair of pieces still agrees.
Instantly, whenever a file those tests care about is changed.
A clear pass or fail result for each check, saved with the code.
Fast, repeatable checks tuned to the project's own common mistakes.
Someone has to write the tests, and keep them current as the code changes shape.

blocks.text: The anti-drift watcher never writes the application itself. It watches the builder, reads the evidence the automatic checks produce, confirms it against the code, and decides whether something is worth flagging. Air traffic control, not a pilot.

blocks.title: Make the save note count (Commit Spine)

blocks.text: Developers already save their work in batches, each with a short note describing it (a commit). This approach makes that note do double duty. The note follows a set template that lists which pieces the change touched and which related pieces were checked. When the developer files the batch, an automatic check makes sure the note is complete before letting it through, and the AI drafts the note from what it actually changed. Because it happens at the moment work is filed away, the record is trustworthy, and over time the project's history becomes a searchable account of what changed and what was kept in step.

blocks.title: Each save, as a record of what was kept in step
blocks.caption: Each saved batch of work lists the pieces it touched. The markers light up as each batch lands, and a tally shows how much of the chain has been kept in step.

blocks.title: Make the save note count, in plain terms
rows: Turns each batch of saved work into a structured note of what changed and what was checked.
At the moment a developer files a batch of work (a commit).
The project's ordinary history, now a searchable record of what stayed in sync.
Almost free to adopt; it reuses a record teams already keep.
It records what was intended at filing time, not whether every piece was truly updated.

blocks.title: An index card per file (Dependency Manifest)

blocks.text: This gives every important file a little index card listing what feeds into it and what depends on it. The card is rebuilt each time the file changes, and a quick check compares the card against the file's actual contents to spot anything inconsistent. A small diagram then shows the file in the middle, with what flows in stacked above and what flows out below, each marked healthy, stale, or broken. Because every relationship is written down plainly, the other checks can read the cards instead of re-reading all the code.

blocks.title: One file, what feeds it and what depends on it
blocks.caption: The changed file sits in the middle, with what feeds into it above and what depends on it below. Each connection is marked healthy, stale, or broken; the broken one gets a note.

blocks.title: An index card per file, in plain terms
rows: Gives each file an index card of what it depends on and what depends on it, and checks the card stays accurate.
Each time a file is changed.
A small, readable card per file, plus a health check.
A clear, file-by-file picture of how things connect that stays readable as the project grows.
The cards can fall behind reality if a relationship is missed; lots of files means lots of cards.

blocks.title: An end-of-step checklist (Drift Sweep)

blocks.text: This runs the person's end-of-step checklist automatically, at the end of every step. It checks the obvious things: does the documentation match the live interface, do the database fields show up where they should, do the training notes describe the current screens? It produces one short report per step. If too much has drifted, it can stop the AI and require the drift be fixed before work continues. It runs once per step rather than on every edit, so its cost is predictable, and it can hold the line by refusing to move on until things are back in agreement.

blocks.title: A health reading at the end of each step
blocks.caption: After each step, the fourteen links are marked clean, warning, or stop-worthy, with the history stacked below. A stop-worthy link must be fixed before the next step.

blocks.title: An end-of-step checklist, in plain terms
rows: Runs the end-of-step checklist automatically and can halt work until drift is fixed.
Once at the end of each step (when the AI stops).
A short health report for each step.
Matching the rhythm people already use, at a predictable cost, and able to stop the line.
It catches drift after the step is done, and a wrong threshold either nags or lets things slip.

blocks.title: The shared dashboard (Tile Board)

blocks.text: This is the dashboard, not a checker. It draws the project as a grid, features down one side and the fourteen links across the top, and colours each square using whatever the other checks have found. It is a single page the team can open to see, at a glance, where everything stands, and it can show the results of any of the other approaches without changing them. Figure 17.7 shows it running live: the builder works, the anti-drift watcher sweeps across, and flagged notes pile up.

blocks.title: The shared dashboard, with a builder and a sweep
blocks.caption: Features down the side, the fourteen links across the top. The builder works through several steps; the watcher then sweeps across, the out-of-date squares light up, and notes collect in the shared folder.

blocks.title: The shared dashboard, in plain terms
rows: Shows the whole project as a colour-coded grid, fed by whatever the other checks find.
Refreshes at the end of each step, or whenever you open it.
Seeing the state of everything at a glance; it is the team's shared view.
It only displays; it needs at least one of the other checks behind it to be useful.

blocks.title: Watchers on the side

blocks.text: One AI builder loaded with every concern at once, drift, security, testing, documentation, writing style, becomes overwhelmed, and its instructions balloon until they stop helping. The cleaner arrangement is to split the jobs. The builder keeps its attention on building. The watchers run separately, beside the project, as their own sessions. Each one quietly observes the project's files, runs its own checks when something changes, and leaves a note, a ticket, in a shared folder. The builder reads those tickets at the start of its next step and handles them like any other task. No watcher shares the builder's working memory, or any other watcher's.

Because the notes are just files in a folder, the whole thing is sturdy: if one watcher has trouble, the builder carries on untouched. Each watcher can run on its own schedule, and even on a cheaper AI for routine checks, saving the strongest model for the hard calls. Adding a new concern means adding a new watcher, not piling more onto the builder. Figure 17.7 is this idea in miniature: the anti-drift watcher observing, sweeping, and leaving notes while the builder works.

blocks.title: How a note travels
rows: A watcher notices something and writes a note.
The builder reads it at the start of a step and marks it in progress.
The builder fixes it, writes how, and files it away.
A weekly tidy-up moves old resolved notes out of the way.
blocks.caption: Each note is a small file recording what changed, the evidence for it, a suggested fix, and which watcher raised it.

blocks.title: Who does what
rows: A shared folder in the project
blocks.caption: The Anti-Drift watcher is one of several. The four review agents it ships with, red, green, blue, and yellow, are described in the [Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents paper](/paper/l199t).

blocks.title: How to combine them, and what is still open

blocks.text: You do not need all seven. The simplest useful pairing is the heat-map plus the end-of-step sweep: one shows where work is happening, the other runs the checklist every step. If you want airtight, repeatable checking, pair the small automatic tests with the dashboard. If you want the broadest coverage, use the walker reading the per-file index cards, with the dashboard showing the result.

blocks.title: Sensible combinations
rows: One shows where work happened; the other runs the checklist at the end of every step.
The tests do the checking the same way every time; the dashboard shows the results, with no AI judgment in the loop.
The cards give the walker a fast map; the walker records what drifted; the dashboard shows it.
Discipline at filing time, plus a free visual log of what happened.

blocks.title: Still open for discussion
rows: Where should the matching rules live?
Tailored to each project, or shared across all of them?
Should the walker rely on fixed rules or AI judgment first?
Fixed rules are cheaper and quieter; judgment is more flexible.
How much drift should stop the line?
Too sensitive and it nags; too loose and things slip through.
One index card per file, or one big map?
At what project size does a single map win?
A fresh dashboard each step, or a live one?

blocks.text: Two principles run through all seven. Do the checking with fixed, repeatable logic wherever possible, and ask the AI to judge only where judgment is genuinely needed. And keep the record inside the project itself, as plain files beside the code, with no extra service to run. It is the same answer the [Compression Problem](/paper/eujjc) reaches from the other side: as a system grows past what any one mind, or any one AI, can hold at once, detail is lost at every handoff, so the discipline that preserves it has to be built into the project rather than left to anyone's memory.
